items.title: iCAUR abbatte i confini e ti porta ovunque
items.description: L'industria globale dell’auto si sta rapidamente orientando verso l'elettrificazione e la personalizzazione. In quanto mercato chiave per i veicoli pr
items.body:
iCAUR abbatte i confini e ti porta ovunque
10 Maggio 2026
 Mercato e News, Eventi
0

L’industria globale dell’auto si sta rapidamente orientando verso l’elettrificazione e la personalizzazione. In quanto mercato chiave per i veicoli premium a energia alternativa, l’Europa è pronta a superare l’omogeneizzazione.
Il marchio premium iCAUR entra nella regione con un nuovo approccio alla mobilità robusta e dalle forme squadrate.
Migliora il comfort dei fuoristrada tradizionali, superando al contempo i limiti delle auto familiari urbane per i viaggi a lunga distanza all’aria aperta, rendendo le esperienze off-road più accessibili. La gioia di vivere all’aperto si evolve dall’esplorazione individuale ai momenti condivisi con familiari e amici: un passaggio da “Per te” a “Per tutti”.
Pensata per uno stile di vita Urban Outdoor, iCAUR combina autonomia elevata, capacità fuoristrada, design spazioso e personalizzazione flessibile. Più che un semplice mezzo di trasporto, offre ai consumatori europei un compagno pratico e al tempo stesso distintivo per l’esplorazione e la libertà.
Con un focus su design e tecnologia, iCAUR si concentra sulla qualità autentica e su esperienze incentrate sull’utente, piuttosto che su un’eccessiva ricerca di specifiche tecniche. La sua silhouette squadrata, audace e minimalista, si adatta sia agli spostamenti urbani che alle esplorazioni all’aria aperta, in linea con le preferenze europee per la praticità e la raffinata semplicità.
iCAUR si rivolge a chi apprezza l’equilibrio, la funzionalità discreta e la libertà di esplorare. Come compagna di vita, consente transizioni fluide tra città e natura, senza compromettere la praticità quotidiana o le ambizioni outdoor.
iCAUR è pensata per l’Europa, con prodotti ottimizzati per le esigenze locali. Le interfacce di modifica standard consentono una facile installazione di attrezzature da campeggio e portapacchi, permettendo un passaggio senza soluzione di continuità tra gli spostamenti quotidiani e le esplorazioni all’aria aperta, dando vita allo stile di vita Urban Outdoor.
iCAUR V27 rafforza questo posizionamento con caratteristiche fondamentali: un’autonomia combinata di oltre 1.000 km, un’altezza da terra di 224 mm per una maggiore sicurezza in fuoristrada e un passo di 2.900 mm per un ampio spazio interno. Un propulsore stabile gestisce con facilità i diversi tipi di terreno, mentre i sistemi intelligenti intuitivi semplificano l’esperienza, favorendo un perfetto equilibrio tra città e natura.
Senza vincoli di tendenza, iCAUR si basa su praticità, semplicità e un approccio incentrato sull’utente, integrando lo stile di vita Urban Outdoor in ogni dettaglio. Pensato per l’Europa, offre una mobilità comoda e versatile senza compromessi.
Fonte: Chery Group

items.description:
Il mercato delle due ruote elettriche, che beneficiano della coda degli incentivi Ecobonus, ha mostrato un settore a due velocità.
 [...]
L'articolo P
items.body:
Positivo il mercato di aprile delle due ruote elettriche
10 Maggio 2026
 Moto e scooter, Veicoli ecologici
0

Il mercato delle due ruote elettriche, che beneficiano della coda degli incentivi Ecobonus, ha mostrato un settore a due velocità.
Crescono le moto elettriche (+150%), mentre flettono i settori dei ciclomotori elettrici (-5,21%) e degli scooter elettrici (-3,57%). Nel cumulato annuo il settore cede 6,56 punti percentuali e registra 2.293 unità.
Il mercato generale del mese di aprile, nonostante le difficoltà di lettura del dato statistico causate dai rimbalzi tecnici legati alla fine dell’omologazione Euro 5 che hanno caratterizzato la prima metà del 2025, nel complesso il mercato delle due ruote a motore mostra un buon andamento, registrando una crescita del 15%.
Le prospettive, tuttavia, restano strettamente legate all’evoluzione del contesto macroeconomico: la durata dell’attuale clima di incertezza, insieme alle ricadute del costo dell’energia e dei carburanti e, più in generale, dell’inflazione, potrebbe incidere sulla capacità di spesa dei consumatori. In questo scenario, appare fondamentale un deciso allentamento delle tensioni geopolitiche per salvaguardare i livelli di consumo. A fronte di queste criticità, prevale comunque un cauto ottimismo. Le due ruote si confermano infatti come la modalità di trasporto più economica e versatile, qualità che le rendono particolarmente competitive in contesti incerti come quello attuale – ha dichiarato il Presidente di ANCMA, Mariano Roman.
In questo quadro si inserisce anche la campagna “Due ruote che valgono doppio”, lanciata da ANCMA lo scorso 23 aprile, che punta a valorizzare il ruolo strategico del settore nella mobilità contemporanea.
Il mercato di aprile
Nel quarto mese dell’anno si registra ancora una crescita a doppia cifra per il mercato delle due ruote a motore, che aumenta i volumi del 14,82% (ma aprile 2025 perdeva 5 punti percentuali) e 44.359 veicoli venduti. Prosegue la marcia degli scooter, che segnano una crescita del 23,25% e 25.971 unità; discreto, ma meno significativo, il risultato delle moto, che incassano un +3,65% e 17.055 mezzi targati; in ripresa anche i ciclomotori, che crescono di 20,42 punti percentuali e 1.333 unità.
Mercato cumulato
Il primo quadrimestre dell’anno chiude con 129.160 registrazioni e una crescita del 14,80%. Anche per effetto dei risultati di aprile, si amplia il divario tra gli scooter e le moto, con i primi che mettono sul mercato 72.357 veicoli e crescono del 17,60%, mentre le seconde si fermano a 52.705 unità e 10,41% punti percentuali di crescita (gli scooter rappresentano ormai il 58% del targato, solo due anni fa si fermavano al 52%); infine i ciclomotori, che recuperano un quarto del loro mercato (+26,33%) e mettono in strada 4.098 mezzi.
Quadricicli
In affanno il mercato dei quadricicli, che ad aprile cala del 33,81% e 1.306 veicoli venduti. Analizzando i segmenti si evince tuttavia che, mentre le minicar a trazione elettrica, attualmente prive degli incentivi Ecobonus, lasciano sul terreno ben 51,73 punti percentuali per un totale di 878 veicoli immessi sul mercato, i termici incassano un incremento del 177,92% e 428 unità.
Nel seguente link potete leggere e/o scaricare i dati del mercato italiano delle due ruote elettriche del mese di aprile 2026. (PDF)
Andamento mercato elettrici

items.title: Al vaglio dell’UE una nuova tipologia di revisione auto: le novità
items.description:
Tecnologia, trasparenza e flessibilità: la nuova bozza UE che riscrive le regole dei controlli periodici per un parco circolante più sicuro
Il post Al
items.body:

Curiosità

Home

    Al vaglio dell'UE una nuova tipologia di revisione auto: le novità

Tecnologia, trasparenza e flessibilità: la nuova bozza UE che riscrive le regole dei controlli periodici per un parco circolante più sicuro

G
                        C
                    
                            
        
        
            
                Giorgio Colari
            
            
                Pubblicato il 9 mag 2026

Aggiungi Autoblog come
        Fonte preferita su Google

La sicurezza sulle strade europee si appresta a vivere una profonda trasformazione tecnologica grazie a una serie di riforme recentemente approvate dalla commissione Trasporti del Parlamento UE. L’obiettivo di queste nuove direttive è quello di modernizzare il processo di revisione dei veicoli, adattandolo a un parco auto sempre più elettronico e connesso.

Uno dei cambiamenti più significativi riguarda l’oggetto stesso dell’ispezione. Le verifiche periodiche non si limiteranno più ai soli componenti meccanici tradizionali, come freni o sterzo, ma si estenderanno ai moderni dispositivi di sicurezza attiva e passiva. Nello specifico, i tecnici dovranno testare il corretto funzionamento dei sistemi ADAS, degli airbag e della frenata automatica d’emergenza, elementi che fino ad oggi non erano inclusi nei controlli standard. Parallelamente, l’evoluzione della mobilità elettrica trova spazio con l’introduzione di prove specifiche per i veicoli a batteria e ibridi, un’operazione che renderà la procedura di revisione più complessa e tecnica rispetto al passato.

Richiami obbligatori vanno rispettati

Un altro requisito fondamentale per ottenere l’idoneità alla circolazione sarà la conformità ai richiami obbligatori delle case madri: se un’auto non ha risposto a una campagna di sicurezza ufficiale, non potrà superare l’ispezione. Per quanto riguarda le emissioni inquinanti, si promuove l’analisi del particolato e degli ossidi di azoto, lasciando però ai singoli Stati membri la facoltà di rendere tali misurazioni obbligatorie o meno.

Nonostante si fosse ipotizzato un inasprimento della frequenza dei controlli per le auto più vecchie, gli eurodeputati hanno deciso di mantenere lo status quo. È stata infatti respinta la proposta di rendere annuale la revisione per i mezzi con oltre dieci anni di anzianità. La commissione ha giudicato tale misura sproporzionata e non supportata da dati certi sulla riduzione della sinistrosità, confermando quindi l’attuale cadenza biennale, pur restando ferma la possibilità per i governi nazionali di applicare regole più severe se necessario.

Revisione valida anche in un altro Paese dell’UE

Una novità pensata per agevolare i cittadini UE che si trovano lontano da casa è la possibilità di effettuare la revisione in un Paese diverso da quello di immatricolazione. Questa procedura comporterà il rilascio di un certificato temporaneo valido per sei mesi, con l’obbligo però di regolarizzare la posizione nel Paese d’origine alla scadenza successiva.

Sul fronte della trasparenza nel mercato dell’usato, la nuova normativa dichiara guerra alla manomissione dei contachilometri. Le officine avranno infatti l’obbligo di registrare il chilometraggio del mezzo per ogni intervento di riparazione che superi l’ora di lavoro. Questi dati verranno poi convogliati dai produttori in un database nazionale, creando una cronologia certificata che scoraggi le frodi.

Novità anche per le due ruote

Infine, le riforme coinvolgono anche i mezzi a due ruote e i veicoli pesanti: le motociclette di cilindrata superiore a 125 cc non godranno più di alcuna flessibilità e dovranno sottoporsi a ispezioni periodiche obbligatorie. Al contempo, si punta a intensificare le verifiche tecniche su strada per autobus e camion, con l’obiettivo di controllare il 5% del parco circolante nazionale, estendendo tali controlli anche ai furgoni.
È importante ricordare che queste misure non sono ancora esecutive. L’iter legislativo prevede un passaggio fondamentale alla fine di maggio, quando il Parlamento Europeo si riunirà in sessione plenaria per il voto definitivo che stabilirà le date di entrata in vigore delle nuove regole.

items.description: Il rapper Ozymandias ha denunciato con un video condiviso nelle stories di Instagram, girato nella stanza di un ospedale, l’aggressione subita nel qua
items.body:
Trending News
        
    
    
    
            “Mi hanno spaccato di botte a San Lorenzo. Mi hanno tirato dei calci in testa. Sono dei pezzi di me*da”: lo sfogo del rapper Ozymandias in ospedale
    
            Il rapper racconta la sua versione dei fatti mentre cercava di sedare una rissa
    
    
            
            di Redazione FqMagazine

10 Maggio 2026

Commenti

Segui FQ su

WhatsApp

Discover

Il rapper Ozymandias ha denunciato con un video condiviso nelle stories di Instagram, girato nella stanza di un ospedale, l’aggressione subita nel quartiere San Lorenzo di Roma: “Mi hanno spaccato di botte sotto la piazza“.
E ancora: “Sono arrivati cinque ragazzi di colore, non ricordo nient’altro. Hanno tirato due pugni in faccia a una persona vicino a me – racconta il rapper – mi sono messo in mezzo per dividerli e mi hanno spaccato di botte”. Mentre era a terra, ricorda, “mi hanno tirato dei calci in testa. Sono dei pezzi di me*da. Ma sono vivo, il mio naso sta bene e anche i denti”. Il mondo, dice nel video, “fa schifo” e “la gente è cattiva, però resistiamo e io resisterò- Pensiamo alle cose belle, ‘Ozylandia’ uscirà il 29 maggio”.
Poi il rapper ha condiviso un altro video: “Io volevo intanto ringraziare tutte le persone che mi hanno scritto, che mi hanno sostenuto. Mi sono veramente sentito voluto bene quando ho messo la storia questa mattina. Amici e fan, veramente vi voglio bene. E niente, è andata che sono arrivati questa dozzina di marocchini a aggredire senza alcun motivo. Amici miei, sono andato a difenderli con calma, cercando ingenuamente di calmarli, dicendo, ‘regà, calma, calma, piano’. Quelli hanno risposto ‘oh tu che cazzo sei!’ pam pam pam tutte botte in faccia”.
E ancora: “Mi è arrivato un calcio in faccia da steso e niente regà questa è la situazione di San Lorenzo al giorno d’oggi è veramente un disagio uno schifo rischi l’aggressione ogni volta che esci è veramente triste. Sono fortunato volevo rassicurare tutti voi che non ho danni al corpo al naso ai denti comunque ho l’occhio sfondato ho lo zigomo sfondato però mi riprenderò. Il male lo rendi meno forte con le cose belle e col bene non è che devi combattere il male con altro male. Secondo me tu neutralizzi il male con la bellezza del bene”.

items.title: From intermittent to persistent chill insufficiency in California’s specialty crops
items.body:
Download PDF

Article
    
        
            Open access

Published: 09 May 2026

From intermittent to persistent chill insufficiency in California’s specialty crops
                    Prakash K. Jha 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-9268-32881, Geruo A 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0001-5714-402X2, Tapan B. Pathak1,3 & …Isabella Velicogna2,4 Show authors

Communications Sustainability

volume 1, Article number: 76 (2026)
            Cite this article

Subjects
        
            AgricultureClimate-change impacts

AbstractSufficient winter chill is critical for breaking dormancy and enabling normal flowering and fruit set in temperature fruit and nut crops. Here, we use observational data to show that increasing interannual temperature variability has already reduced winter chill in California’s Central Valley, contrary to previous projections that chill insufficiency would occur only by the mid-to-late 21st century. Inadequate chill is affecting crops such as pistachio and plum, particularly in Southern California. We evaluate adaptation strategies using subseasonal temperature forecasts to assess chill sufficiency by early February and guide dormancy-breaking treatments in low-chill years. Using this approach, we demonstrate that a simple in-season forecasting strategy, driven by subseasonal temperature forecasts, can predict chill accumulation within 10 percent of observed winter chill in 89–94 percent of cases. We also propose revised chill thresholds to inform breeding programs for low-chill varieties and guide management interventions to reduce risks in low-chill years.

Similar content being viewed by others
                
                    
                        
                            
                                
                                    
                                
                                
                                    
                                        Seasonal climate forecasts show skill in predicting winter chill for specialty crops in California

                                         Open access

                                        Agro-pastoralists’ perception of climate change and adaptation in the Qilian Mountains of northwest China
                                        
                                    
                                    
                                        Article
                                         Open access
                                         25 July 2022
                                    
                                
                            
                        
                    
                        
                            
                                
                                    
                                
                                
                                    
                                        Impacts of cold stress and temperature fluctuations on bud abortion and malformed flower development on Hayward kiwifruit
                                        
                                    
                                    
                                        Article
                                         Open access
                                         25 December 2025
                                    
                                
                            
                        
                    
                
            
        
            
                window.dataLayer = window.dataLayer || [];
                window.dataLayer.push({
                    recommendations: {
                        recommender: 'semantic',
                        model: 'e5',
                        policy_id: null,
                        timestamp: 1778425902,
                        embedded_user: 'null'
                    }
                });
            
        
    
                
                
                
                    
                        IntroductionWinter chill accumulation, defined as the cumulative exposure to cool temperatures during dormancy, is a key regulator of phenological development in temperate fruit and nut trees1,2 and is commonly quantified as chill portions1,3 (CP) or chill hours4 (CH). These crops require sufficient chill to transition out of winter dormancy and initiate their growth cycle. Each crop has specific chill requirements for proper development, known as its chill threshold. For instance, satisfactory growth typically requires approximately 38–54 CP for walnuts (Juglans regia), 36–65 for pistachios (Pistacia vera), 30–70 for cherries (Prunus spp.), and 55–60 for plums (Prunus spp.), depending on the variety5. Because many commercial cultivars of these crops operate near their chilling thresholds, modest reductions in winter chill (e.g., 5–10 CP) can be sufficient to shift a season from adequate to insufficient chilling. Inadequate chill can disrupt winter dormancy release and subsequent phenological development, leading to delayed flowering, irregular bud break, prolonged bloom periods, and result in uneven fruit development and ripening6. These disruptions reduce fruit quality, extend harvest periods, and lead to substantial yield losses. Conversely, when the chill requirements are fulfilled too early and are followed by unseasonably warm temperatures, bud break may occur prematurely, increasing the vulnerability of tender buds to spring frost damage7. Given these challenges, accurate chill prediction is critical for planning management interventions, such as the use of dormancy-breaking agents to promote uniform bud break under insufficient chill and frost protection strategies. Here, we evaluate the potential of subseasonal to seasonal forecasts (S2S) to provide chill sufficiency information with lead times of up to one season in advance.Concerns have intensified over declining chill accumulation in regions with Mediterranean-type climates characterized by relatively mild winters, such as California. Unlike cooler temperate regions, California operates within a narrow margin, where the difference between the required and available chill is small, leaving its specialty crops vulnerable to even a modest increase in temperature8. Several studies have documented this decline in chill. For example, Baldocchi and Wong8 estimated a loss of ~40 CH per decade. Luedeling et al9. projected that winter chill in California’s Central Valley could decline by 30–60% by mid-century and up to 80% by the end of the century, relative to the 700–1200 CH observed around 1950. Such reductions could push many regions below the physiological thresholds needed for successful bud break, thereby reducing flowering and fruit set10. These studies robustly document long-term declines in winter chill but largely emphasize mean trends rather than year-to-year variability.Crucially, climate change is not only causing gradual warming but also amplifying the interannual variability of temperature11,12,13, a trend often overlooked in agriculture impact studies. This dual challenge—declining mean and rising variability in chill accumulation—narrows the buffer that historically protected growers from occasional low-chill years. In the past, when available chill exceeded crop requirements by a wide margin, minor forecasting errors had relatively limited consequences for crops needing a high amount of winter chill. Today, however, the margin is shrinking, and unreliable forecasts may result in costly management missteps.Despite extensive documentation of long-term declines in winter chill9,14,15, two critical gaps remain. First, most prior studies focus on mean trends, leaving year-to-year variability largely unquantified, even though it directly determines how frequently chill requirements are not met. Second, it is unclear whether widely used Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP) climate models capture not only the magnitude of observed chill declines but also their interannual variability. Addressing these gaps is essential for distinguishing long-term climate-driven chill risk from operational risk and for evaluating the reliability of climate information used in agricultural decision-making. These gaps motivate the present study, which integrates observed variability, climate projections, and S2S forecasts to assess winter chill risk across decision-relevant timescales.Year-to-year chill variability is often governed by minimum daily temperatures during the coldest portions of the diurnal cycle, so monthly averages may obscure short-lived cold extremes critical for crop physiology. For example, Jha and Pathak16 used monthly-averaged forecasts to evaluate chill variability, but such approaches may inadequately capture these extremes. This highlights the need to evaluate chill accumulation using daily-resolution observations and forecasts.Understanding long-term trends and interannual variability in winter chill is especially important for perennial crops, which require several years to reach full productivity and remain in production for decades. Aligning orchard lifespans and breeding timelines with projected changes in chill underscores the importance of targeting appropriate chill requirements in new cultivars, motivating the need for reliable projections of future chill conditions. Innovative approaches such as Speed Breeding, which uses controlled environmental conditions to accelerate breeding cycles, can shorten breeding timelines and support faster development of climate-adapted cultivars, particularly when integrated with marker-assisted selection and genomic tools17.More generally, adaptation strategies that integrate climate information with crop phenology and management can help mitigate climate change impacts on perennial crops18.In this study, we analyze 44 years of observed daily temperatures from GRIDMET (hereafter referred to as the observational data) to quantify both long-term trend and interannual variability in winter chill accumulation across major fruit and nut-producing regions in California. We evaluate whether current conditions are already approaching or crossing crop-specific chill thresholds by comparing the observed trends and interannual variability in winter chill with CMIP6 projections under a high-emissions pathway. This comparison allows us to assess whether the magnitude and interannual variability of observed declines are consistent with projections under an upper-bound warming scenario and whether the earliest emergence of chill-related risk aligns with timelines reported in previous studies.To assess whether state-of-the-art forecast systems can help growers prepare for the risk of chill deficit, we evaluate the performance of S2S forecasts from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF). Specifically, we examine how well the forecasts capture interannual variability in winter chill accumulation at both monthly and daily temporal resolutions, and how accurately they identify years with insufficient chill.Together, these analyses go beyond long-term trends to quantify how increasing interannual variability alters the frequency of years falling below critical chill thresholds, thereby complementing long-term climate projections by revealing near-term chill-related risk. The study focuses on high-value fruit and nut crops for which California is a leading U.S. producer, including pistachios, walnuts, plums, and cherries, all of which require substantial winter chill for optimal development19. These crops are not only critical to California’s agricultural economy but also play a very important role in national and global food supply chains.ResultsObserved trends in chill accumulationAnalysis of the observational data reveals a notable decline in chill accumulation across major crop-growing regions in California over recent decades. During the baseline period of 1980–1990, average CP in southern California (below 37° latitude) was approximately 64 CP in pistachio and cherry-growing regions, and 65 in walnut and plum regions, with interannual variability reflected by standard deviations (SDs) of 4–4.5 CP and 95% confidence intervals ranging from about 61 to 68 CP across regions. Over the most recent decade (2015–2024), these values declined to roughly 60 CP in all four crop regions, with higher interannual variability (SDs of 6–6.4 CP, 95% CI: ~55–65 CP), representing a decrease of about 4–6 CPs relative to the baseline period (Figs. 1 and 2; and Supplementary Figs. 1 and 2). Linear trend analysis indicates a statistically significant downward trajectory in chill accumulation across all four crop-growing regions (Fig. 2), reflecting the influence of long-term warming. Based on crop-specific chill thresholds, calculated as the median of CP across all listed varieties by Pope5 (58 for pistachios, 47.5 for walnuts, 48 for cherries, and 57.5 for plums), the current trend suggests that many regions are approaching the minimum required chill thresholds for these crops. This poses a concern as full yield potential with quality fruit for these crops cannot be realized without sufficient chill accumulation.Fig. 1: Trends in winter chill changes across California from 1980 to 2023.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageThe map shows the rate of change (slope) in winter chill over the period 1980–2023 at each grid point, estimated using the Theil–Sen estimator. Statistical significance was assessed using the Mann–Kendall test (p < 0.05), and only statistically significant grid points are shown. Positive trends (increasing chill) are shown in teal, and negative trends (decreasing chill) are shown in brown/orange.Fig. 2: Winter chill accumulation in crop-growing regions of southern California.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageTotal winter chill accumulation during the November–December–January–February (NDJF) season, aggregated over crop-growing areas south of 37° latitude in California, for pistachio (a), walnut (b), cherry (c), and plum (d). The blue solid line represents chill derived from the observational dataset (1979–2023), with the corresponding linear trend. The black dashed line shows historical chill from CMIP6 temperature simulations (1981–2014), with a fitted linear trend, and the green solid line shows projected chill for 2015–2040 based on CMIP6 temperature projections, also with a linear trend. The gray-shaded area indicates the range of chill thresholds across cultivars, and the red dashed line represents the median chill threshold. Chill is computed separately using temperature outputs from each of the three global climate models (ACCESS-CM2, EC-Earth3, EC-Earth3-Veg), and results are then averaged. Future projections are based on the SSP 5-8.5 Shared Socioeconomic Pathway.Spatially, the decline in chill accumulation over the period 1980–2024 is widespread across all four crop-growing regions in California, with 91%–95% of grid cells within each crop region showing negative trends in chill accumulation over time, based on Sen’s Slope Estimator20. Statistical significance of these trends was assessed using the autocorrelation-adjusted Mann–Kendell test21,22. The proportion of grid cells showing significant declines (p < 0.05) in CP is 50% in pistachio regions, 57% in walnut regions, 53% in cherry regions, and 54% in plum regions.Rising interannual variabilityImportantly, here we find the emergence of a higher interannual variability regime in chill accumulation since the late 1990s, alongside long-term declines in mean chill. This is reflected in the 10-year moving SD of chill accumulation, which is substantially higher in recent decades than in the 1990s (Fig. 3) and remains persistently elevated thereafter. Although variability appears relatively stable after ~1998, the median rolling SD increased from approximately 4.3–4.6 during the 1990s to 7.1–7.2 in the post-1998 periods across all crops (Mann–Whitney test, p < 0.01), corresponding to an increase of about 56%. This dual pattern of declining means and increasing variability increases the likelihood of years falling below the physiological thresholds required for optimal development of these crops. While linear trend-based trajectories suggest that median chilling thresholds may not be consistently crossed until later decades, the proximity of current chill availability to these thresholds already implies heightened vulnerability to episodic chill insufficiency under present-day climate conditions. Such risk has indeed materialized: inadequate chill accumulation (i.e., with CP falling below its critical median threshold) was observed in pistachio, cherry, and plum-growing regions during only 3, 0, and 2 years, respectively, in the first half of the observational record (1979–1998). In contrast, these numbers increased to 6, 1, and 5 years, respectively, during the latter half of the record (2004–2023; Fig. 2). Although inadequate chill has not been observed for walnuts, due to their relatively low chill requirement, years with chill accumulation just 5 CPs above threshold occurred only once before 1998, but the frequency has tripled in the years since. These results indicate that chill disruptions associated with low chill accumulations are already occurring due to increasing interannual variability and are becoming more frequent across California, particularly for pistachio and plum-growing regions, which experienced insufficient chill in nearly 30% and 25% of the years, respectively, over the past two decades, even though long-term trend-based threshold crossings are projected to occur later. Together, these results highlight that changes in variability play a critical role in determining when and how frequently chill insufficiency occurs and, therefore, must be considered alongside mean trends when evaluating climate change impacts on perennial cropping systems.Fig. 3: Increasing interannual variability in CP in crop-growing regions of California.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageThe figure shows changes in interannual variability of CP for crop-growing regions south of 37° latitude in California, based on GRIDMET data. At each time step, the SD of CP was computed across all grid points within the region using a 10-year moving window.Climate model simulations underestimating the riskWe evaluate whether long-term changes in the frequency of chill insufficiency risk are captured in the climate simulations from the latest generation of Climate Model Intercomparison Project, phase 6 (CMIP6). Because chill accumulation within a given year is strongly influenced by weather variability, our analysis focuses on changes in the frequency of chill insufficiency rather than predicting exact within-year outcomes. Across all four crop-growing regions, CMIP6 historical simulations (1981–2014) substantially underestimate interannual variability in chill accumulation, with the models’ SD roughly half that of observations (about 53% lower). As a result, CMIP6 models fail to reproduce a large fraction of observed chill insufficiency events at annual timescales when evaluated against median cultivar-specific chill requirements. In the historical period, CMIP6 captures only 0–2 insufficient chill years across regions, compared to 2–6 observed events depending on crop, indicating that roughly 60–70% of insufficiency occurrences are missed (Fig. 2). Because the median chill thresholds of walnut and cherry are lower than for pistachio and plum, historical chill insufficiency has been rare, occurring only once in cherry and not at all in walnut during the historical period.Climate projections under SSP5-8.5 starting in 2015 indicate a marked increase in chill insufficiency risk. Pistachio and plum-growing regions experience frequent insufficiency, occurring in 18–19 of the 26 years between 2015 and 2040, whereas walnut and cherry regions begin to show occasional insufficiency (one year by 2039), suggesting that these traditionally low-risk crops may transition toward more frequent chill insufficiency in the early 2040s or beyond if current trends persist (Fig. 2). Importantly, by analyzing GRIDMET observations, we find that these thresholds are already being crossed intermittently for pistachio, cherry, and plum under present-day climate variability, although trend-based analysis indicates that the transition to persistent chill insufficiency occurs later than indicated by isolated low-chill years (Fig. 2). This discrepancy arises because chill accumulation depends on short-term variability, which is not fully represented in CMIP6 model simulations when analyzed at annual timescale. Since CMIP6 models underestimate the observed interannual variability in chill accumulation, projections based solely on mean trend threshold crossings likely underrepresent the near-term frequency of insufficient chill years, particularly in walnut growing regions. Prior works based on climate projections have suggested that the ongoing warming will significantly reduce the accumulation of winter chill in California by mid- to end-century, with conditions expected to no longer support some of the key tree crops currently grown in the region9.Our results refine this interpretation by distinguishing between episodic chill insufficiency that is already occurring due to interannual variability and the longer-term shift toward persistent insufficiency indicated by declining trends. Together, these findings indicate that mid- to late-century timelines may underestimate the urgency of the problem, as physiologically disruptive declines in winter chill are already emerging prior to mid-century and are projected to intensify under continued warming, even if trend-based thresholds are crossed later in the century.We further investigate whether these trends are consistent across administrative boundaries. Analysis at the county level reveals a broadly similar pattern: insufficient chill accumulation has become more frequent across most counties over the past two decades (Supplementary Fig. 5).The finding has important implications for both agricultural adaptation and the use of climate model projections for agricultural risk assessment. First, it indicates that increased interannual variability can lead to earlier occurrences of chill insufficiency than would be expected from long-term mean trends alone. As a result, growers may need to implement adaptation strategies earlier than suggested by mean climate projections. These may include selecting low chill cultivars, applying dormancy-breaking agents, or considering shifts in production regions to maintain crop viability under a warming and more variable climate. Second, this suggests that CMIP6-based projections may underestimate near-term chill risk if variability at the colder end of the temperature distribution (e.g., below 7 °C) is not adequately captured.Evaluation of ECMWF’s forecast skill for chill predictionOur analysis shows that 1-month lead forecasts (NDJ observations + February forecasts) are highly accurate, with predicted chill in the four crop-growing regions within ±10% of observed values for 42 of 43 years using ECMWF daily forecasts (MD) and 41 of 43 years using monthly mean forecasts (MM) (Supplementary Fig. S6). Applying a stricter ±5% threshold reduces the number of accurate years to 30 (MD) and 28 (MM). Grid-level evaluation followed a similar pattern, with 89–91% of grids within ±10%, dropping to 57–60% within ±5%. For 2-month lead forecasts (ND observations + January–February forecasts), accuracy declined to 39 (MD) and 35 (MM) years within ±10%, and only 26 (MD) and 23 (MM) years within ±5%. Longer lead times (3 and 4 months) further reduce performance at both regional and grid levels, with grid-level accuracy dropping from 55–73% (±10%) to 31–44% (±5%).The time series comparison of ECMWF hindcasts and GRIDMET-derived CP illustrates year-to-year differences between predicted and observed values (Supplementary Figs. 7 and 8). It is possible to observe how 1-month lead forecasts closely track observed winter (November–December–January–February (NDJF)), CP across all regions, while forecast skill decreases with longer lead times.The anomaly correlation coefficient (ACC) between NDJF total CP—computed as the ensemble mean of daily forecasts from individual ECMWF members, initialized on 1 November (lead time extending to 4 months)—and corresponding estimates from the observed temperature data ranged from 0.54 to 0.57 across the four crop-growing regions. When using monthly ECMWF forecasts, with CP similarly averaged across all ensemble members, the ACC values are even lower, ranging from 0.48 to 0.52. These relatively modest correlations indicate that the ECMWF forecasting system has limited skill in capturing the interannual variability in chill for these crop-growing regions in California. We also computed ACC between monthly total chill from the ECMWF temperature forecasts and the observed data for lead months 1–4 (November to February), separately (Fig. 4). In the four crop-growing regions (pistachio, walnut, cherry, and plum), 94–95% of locations show ACC > 0.5 at the one-month lead (November), but this drops to only 1–3% in two-month lead (December), 0–7% in three-month lead (January), and to zero thereafter, indicating no usable skill beyond one month.Fig. 4: ACC between the total winter chill derived from the ECMWF forecast and observed temperature.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageThe figure shows the ACC between the monthly total chill derived from ECMWF forecasts and observed temperatures for November through February. The top row shows chill derived from ECMWF direct daily forecasts, and the bottom row shows chill derived from ECMWF daily temperatures reconstructed from ECMWF monthly mean temperatures. ACC is defined as Pearson’s correlation coefficient computed on anomalies and is shown only where statistically significant (p < 0.05). Only regions common to pistachio, walnut, cherry, and plum are displayed.The limited gains in skill despite leveraging both increased temporal granularity and ensemble averaging suggest that improving forecasts of chill will likely require more than just higher temporal resolution or ensemble spread. One possible explanation for the limited skill is that dynamic forecasting systems often rely heavily on ENSO as a source of predictability, even though ENSO has minimal direct influence on subseasonal climate variability in California23,24. Even in strong ENSO years, California’s climate anomalies often deviate substantially from expected patterns. We analyze the correlation between winter chill accumulation in four crop-growing regions and the Oceanic Niño Index (ONI) at various lead and lag intervals. Our results showed that chill accumulation during the NDJF period exhibits a weak positive correlation with ONI values from the next year’s December–March period (correlation coefficients ranging from 0.16 to 0.24), and a weak negative correlation with ONI values from the subsequent September-December period (correlation coefficients ranging from −0.20 to −0.26). In contrast, correlations with ONI values from the concurrent or previous year are generally weaker. We note that nonlinear ENSO effects, ENSO Modoki events, and multi-index predictors may also influence subseasonal climate variability in California, representing promising avenues for improving S2S forecast skill in future work.DiscussionTackling the sooner-than-expected decline in winter chillUnderstanding the implications of a sooner-than-expected decline in winter chill for the future cultivation of these crops in California is crucial. Addressing this challenge requires first understanding how reduced chill accumulation is impacting crop performance, how growers are currently responding, and evaluating the potential effectiveness of available adaptation strategies. Our study contributes to that effort by offering insights that can inform and enhance ongoing work in this area.In particular, our findings suggest that focusing solely on long-term mean declines may underestimate near-term agricultural risk. By showing that increasing interannual variability is already increasing the frequency of insufficient chill years, this study highlights a pathway through which chill insufficiency is emerging earlier than indicated by mean-based projections alone.Current varieties of major tree crops have limited tolerance for winter chill deficiency, with evidence suggesting they may endure up to about a 20 percent decline25. Historically, unusually warm winters, particularly in 1998 and 2013–2014, when CP dropped by about 25%, have already proved to exert significant impacts on crop production and revenue losses in the Central Valley25. As a result, cultivar innovation has emerged as a key adaptation strategy, particularly for high-chill crops. In cherries, experiments are underway to assess the sensitivity of existing cultivars such as Bings, Corals, Royal Hazel, Royal Lynn, Rainier, and Black Pearl located in the southern San Joaquin Valley to declining winter chill26. At the same time, lower-chill varieties such as Royal Lee, Minnie Royal, and Cristobalina are increasingly adopted in warmer regions, especially in Southern California27,28. Pistachio breeding efforts have produced cultivars like Golden Hills and Lost Hills with slightly reduced chill needs, inferred from the earlier bloom dates, but this has not been definitively established (UCCE Farm Advisor Phoebe E Gordon, personal communication). Walnut adaptation via cultivar replacement is slower, likely reflecting the historically low occurrence of chill insufficiency. This slower pace of adaptation is also reflected in the fact that cultivar selection in walnut has been primarily driven by yield, nut quality, and disease avoidance traits, with established cultivars such as Chandler remaining dominant in California (Pers. Comm. with UC ANR Orchard Advisor Kat Jarvis-Shean).Perennial orchard systems operate on multi-decadal timescales, with trees typically requiring several years to reach full production and remaining productive for decades. For example, pistachio, walnut, plum, and cherry trees reach peak production after approximately 7–25 years, while orchard lifespan often extends several decades, depending on the crop29,30,31,32,33. In parallel, the development of new cultivars requires 15–20 years when multiple breeding cycles are involved34. Given these multi-decadal timelines, cultivars initiated today would likely reach commercial production around 2040–2050, when chill accumulation is projected to average approximately 50 CP in these crop-growing regions. Breeding efforts should therefore prioritize perennial varieties' resilience to such low-chill and increasingly variable conditions.While chill thresholds are used here as indicators of dormancy fulfillment and risk, it is important to recognize that chilling and subsequent heat accumulation (forcing) interact in determining phenological development, with a well-established tradeoff between chilling and forcing requirements35,36. Incorporating this interaction would provide a more comprehensive representation of phenological responses, but it is beyond the scope of the present study.Despite this limitation, increasing interannual variability and the resulting uncertainty in chill accumulation highlight the need for actionable, in-season decision-support tools. In-season chill forecasts can offer an additional short-term adaptation tool, enabling growers to anticipate chill shortfalls and plan for the application of rest-breaking agents (Pers. Comm. with UC ANR Orchard Advisor Kat Jarvis-Shean). In California, cherry growers have already been using chill forecast tools, which work better for deciding when to apply dormancy-breaking agents9. Experiments are also ongoing to understand the effectiveness of rest-breaking agents for walnuts in different orchards under low-chill years37. For pistachios, chill forecasts are occasionally used to assess male–female bloom synchronization risks.Under low chill conditions, dormancy-breaking treatments typically begin by at least a month before the bud break37, often by the end of January. This operational timeline makes forecasts with at least a 1-month lead time essential for decision-making. Our results indicate that a practical approach for in-season chill assessment is to combine observed chill accumulation through January with 1-month ECMWF forecasts for February. This hybrid approach leverages the higher skill of short-lead forecasts (e.g., higher ACC and lower RMSE) while retaining observed variability earlier in the season. Using this method, NDJF chill totals were within 10% of observed values in nearly all years (41–42 years out of 43), providing a reliable basis for forecasting chill conditions. Although longer-lead forecasts are desirable for operational decisions, their skill is currently limited; therefore, this 1-month lead hybrid approach offers a near-term, operationally feasible solution for growers.The chill modeling strategy we present here, forecasting chill one month in advance, allows growers to adaptively manage chill shortfalls and make informed decisions on rest-breaking treatments and other nuanced orchard management practices. For instance, delaying pruning can help avoid stimulating premature bud break in years with low chill accumulation38. Similarly, forecasts can inform irrigation scheduling and orchard floor management strategies aimed at modulating microclimates39,40. While some cherry growers have adopted microclimate-modifying practices such as shade netting (e.g., DayChill) to enhance chill accumulation, similar applications remain limited for walnuts and pistachios26,41. Finally, the updated information on long-term chill decline in the future from this study can support broader strategic planning, such as the potential relocation of orchards. These projections make it clear that relocation decisions should not be viewed as a distant future concern. These management and adaptation options are presented to illustrate the range of responses to declining winter chill, a quantitative comparison of their costs, feasibility, or long-term effectiveness is beyond the scope of this study.MethodsStudy areaOur analysis focuses on regions across California where walnuts, pistachios, cherries, and plums are cultivated (Fig. 5). We assessed chill sufficiency separately for each crop, specific to its growing areas. To delineate these areas, we used the 2022 Cropland Data Layer (CDL) from USDA-NASS, which provides land use data at a 30 m resolution42. Crop-Specific masks were created by aggregating the CDL data to align with the 4 km resolution of the observational data. Chill sufficiency computation was then generated for each masked region at this 4 km resolution. Additionally, we emphasized crop-growing regions in southern California (south of 37° latitude), where previous studies have reported chill insufficiency risks16,25. A county-level analysis was also conducted for the six counties with the largest cultivation areas for these crops.Fig. 5: Spatial distribution of crop-growing regions for walnut, pistachio, cherry, and plum in California.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageColored areas in the map represent regions where each crop is grown. The six counties with the largest crop-growing area for each crop are highlighted with bold blue outlines.DataDaily maximum (Tmax) and minimum (Tmin) temperatures from the observational dataset (GRIDMET) were used as the reference for historical observations in this study. GRIDMET provides temperature data at a 4 km spatial resolution, available from 1979 up to the previous day. The dataset is developed by merging temporally rich data from the North American Land Data Assimilation System Phase 2 (NLDAS-2) with the spatially fine resolution (800-m) Parameter-elevation Regressions on Independent Slopes Model (PRISM) data43. It was validated against an extensive network of automated weather stations across the western United States, showing skill comparable to station-based interpolation methods. For consistency with the model forecast period, we selected data from 1981 to 2024.Model data from the ECMWF, consisting of six-hourly forecasts, were averaged to obtain daily values for the November–December–January–February (NDJF) period. Forecasts were initialized on the first day of each month (November, December, January, and February) from 1981 to 2024 (https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/seasonal-original-single-levels?tab=download), including both 1-month lead forecasts (e.g., November initialized in November) and longer lead times up to 4 months depending on the initialization month. Thus, the lead time varied by forecast start date, ranging from near-zero (same-month initialization) to approximately 4 months in advance. Additionally, monthly mean forecasts for the NDJF season were obtained from ECMWF for the same years from one to four-month lead times (https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/seasonal-monthly-single-levels?tab=download). We chose ECMWF due to its widely recognized performance, as previous studies have established it as the gold standard for medium-range weather forecasting44.The ECMWF hindcasts are available at a spatial resolution of 1° × 1° (~111 km). However, at this coarse resolution, the model data are limited in their ability to capture temperature variations influenced by topography, proximity to coastlines, and other meso-climatic factors. In contrast, the observational dataset offers finer spatial detail and can represent these small-scale temperature variations more accurately, which is critical for realistic predictions of winter chill. To leverage the large-scale forecast signal while retaining fine-scale variability, we apply the ECMWF anomalies to the 4 km observational climatology using both mean and SD scaling. In this approach, the coarse-scale forecast anomaly is applied across the high-resolution grid, while the local variability from observations is explicitly preserved. This SD-ratio scaling improves chill estimation relative to using raw forecasts or mean-only bias correction. Our aim is not to predict exact daily chill at individual locations, but to capture anomalies in chill sufficiency at monthly-to-seasonal scales for crop-growing regions. This method has been successfully applied in previous studies16,45,46.To compute CP, hourly temperatures are required, which were derived from daily maximum (Tmax) and minimum (Tmin) temperatures. The daily forecasts of Tmax and Tmin from ECMWF were bias-corrected using the nearest observational grid point and scaled through SD-ratio adjustment to match the observed variance.$${T}_{{max} {D}}^{{BC}}={T}_{max }^{{obsDClim}}+{T}_{max }^{{modDAnom}}\cdot \frac{{\sigma }_{{T}_{max D}}^{{obs}}}{{\sigma }_{{T}_{max D}}^{{mod}}}$$

(1) and (2), \({T}_{\max D}^{{{\mathrm{BC}}}}\) and \({T}_{\min D}^{{{\mathrm{BC}}}}\) represent the bias-corrected daily maximum and minimum temperatures from ECMWF daily forecasts at a spatial resolution of the observational data, around 4 km for each lead time. The daily climatology \({T}_{\max }^{{{\mathrm{obsDClim}}}}\) and \({T}_{\min }^{{{\mathrm{obsDClim}}}}\) in the observational dataset were derived by averaging daily values for each calendar day over the period 1981–2024. \({T}_{\max }^{{{\mathrm{modDAnom}}}}\) and \({T}_{\min }^{{{\mathrm{modDAnom}}}}\) refer to the daily anomalies of ECMWF’s Tmax and Tmin, respectively. The daily anomalies were computed by subtracting the corresponding daily climatological means from the ECMWF hindcasts. The terms \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\max D}}^{{{\mathrm{obs}}}}\) and \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\min D}}^{{{\mathrm{obs}}}}\) denote the daily SDs of maximum and minimum temperatures from the observation, while \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\max D}}^{{{\mathrm{mod}}}}\) and \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\min D}}^{{{\mathrm{mod}}}}\) represent the corresponding SDs from the ECMWF forecasts. The daily SD of the corresponding variables was computed for each calendar day over the entire period.Since the observational data were available at a finer spatial resolution (4 km) compared to the coarser resolution of the ECMWF forecasts (1° × 1°), it was necessary to align their resolutions to perform the arithmetic operations in the equation. To achieve this, we resampled the ECMWF forecasts to match the resolution of observational data by replicating each grid cell across the corresponding observational data grids.The monthly forecasts of Tmax and Tmin from ECMWF were then downscaled to daily values using observational data from the nearest grid point to each ECMWF grid cell. Bias and variance scaling (SD-ratio) was applied in this process to preserve the observed interannual variability while retaining the large-scale anomaly signal. This is expressed as:$${T}_{max M}^{{BC}}={T}_{max }^{{obsDClim}}+{T}_{max }^{{modMAnom}}\cdot \frac{{\sigma }_{{T}_{max M}}^{{obs}}}{{\sigma }_{{T}_{max M}}^{{mod}}}$$

(3) and (4), \({T}_{\max M}^{{{\mathrm{BC}}}}\) and \({T}_{\min M}^{{{\mathrm{BC}}}}\) refer to the daily maximum and minimum temperatures generated from ECMWF monthly forecasts at a spatial resolution of the observational data, 4 km for each lead time. \({T}_{\max }^{{{\mathrm{modMAnom}}}}\) and \({T}_{\min }^{{{\mathrm{modMAnom}}}}\) are the monthly anomalies of Tmax and Tmin from ECMWF, calculated by subtracting their respective climatology from the ECMWF hindcasts. The terms \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\max M}}^{{{\mathrm{mod}}}}\) and \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\min M}}^{{{\mathrm{mod}}}}\) denote the monthly SDs of Tmax and Tmin from the ECMWF forecasts, while \({\sigma }_{{T}_{{{\mathrm{maxM}}}}}^{{{\mathrm{obs}}}}\) and \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\min M}}^{{{\mathrm{obs}}}}\) represent the corresponding SDs from the reference data.Hourly temperatures needed for chill computation were derived from the daily Tmax and Tmin, latitude, and sunrise and sunset times. Daytime warming was modeled using a sine curve, while nighttime cooling was modeled with a logarithmic function, following the methods in the ChillR package developed by Luedeling et al47. To evaluate whether deriving hourly temperatures from daily Tmax and Tmin introduces substantial uncertainty in chill accumulation, we computed annual total CP using both methods for three representative stations (Modesto, Davis, and Five Points) using California Irrigation Management System (CIMIS) data (https://cimis.water.ca.gov/). The annual totals from these two approaches were computed using paired t-tests and Wilcoxon tests (implemented in R), which indicated no significant differences, confirming that the derived hourly temperatures provided a reliable estimate of chill accumulation.Chill quantificationChill units are a thermal-time metric quantifying exposure to effective chilling temperatures, and dormancy is completed once sufficient chill units have accumulated48. It can be measured as one hour at an optimal temperature, as in the Chilling Hours model, or a more complex unit of accumulated chill, as in the Dynamic Model. The total chilling requirement reflects the number of units or hours accumulated within a specified temperature range, which varies by species and cultivars49. Chill accumulation can be quantified using the Chilling Hours model, which counts the number of hours with temperatures between 0 °C and 7.2 °C50, while ignoring all other temperatures, or by calculating CP using the dynamic model1,3. The dynamic model is considered particularly well-suited for warming climates, such as that of California, because it represents chill accumulation in a more physiologically based manner than simpler chill-hour models and partially accounts for the reduction of chilling effectiveness at higher temperatures51,52,53.We quantified chill accumulation using CP, calculated with the Dynamic_model function from the ChillR package47. The dynamic model estimates CP through a two-step process: first, exposure to cold temperatures leads to the formation of an intermediate product, which can either be destroyed by subsequent warm temperatures or strengthened by moderate temperatures. Once a specific quantity of this intermediate product accumulates, it is permanently converted into a CP, unaffected by later temperature changes. The model needs hourly temperature data (in degrees Celsius) over a specified time period and applies experimentally derived constants within an exponential equation to compute the cumulative CP across the entire duration.We computed CP for the NDJF season total, as well as monthly total for the entire California using observational data, and ECMWF’s monthly and daily hindcasts for the period 1981–2024. For each season, chill accumulation from November and December of a given year was combined with January and February of the following year (e.g., November–December 1981 with January–February 1982). Chill computation of the 2024–2025 season was not possible, as January and February data for 2025 were unavailable at the time of this study.Analysis of CPWe computed the slope of the interannual total CP in the observational data between 1980 and 2023. For each grid point, the trend slope of total CP was estimated using Sen’s Slope Estimator20. This method computes the slope for each pair of data points as the ratio of the difference in chill values (y) to the difference in time (x). The final Sen’s Slope is the median of all pairwise slopes, providing a robust estimate of trend magnitude that is less sensitive to outliers or non-normal distributions than a simple least squares linear fit. The statistical significance of the trends (1980–2023) was assessed using the autocorrelation-adjusted Mann–Kendall test (Yue–Pilon modification)21,22. Both the trend slope and modified significance were computed in Python using the pymannkendall package.A trend was considered statistically significant if the p-value was less than 0.05. We then clipped the crop-growing regions on the gridded chill data, to determine the proportion of each region experiencing significant chill decline. The proportion of each region experiencing a significant decline was calculated by dividing the number of significant grid cells within the region by the total number of grid cells in that region, then multiplying by 100. We also calculated chill for Southern California (south of 37° latitude) and for individual counties by averaging chill values across all grid cells within the latitude band or county boundaries.We computed the statistical significance of the difference in the coefficient of variation (CV) of CP between the first (1980–1989) and last (2014–2023) decades. The CV was computed separately for each period, and a statistical comparison was conducted using an independent t-test54,55. The difference was considered significant if the p-value was less than 0.05. Additionally, we assessed the interannual variability by computing the SD of CP using a 10-year moving window for each crop-growing region.To examine whether CMIP6 projections capture the observed decline in chill accumulation, we calculated CP using Tmax and Tmin projections from Global Climate Models (GCMs) participating in Coupled Model Intercomparison Project phase 6 (CMIP6) experiments for both historical (1981–2014) and future (2015–2100) periods for the entire California. We selected three GCMS — ACCESS-CM2, EC-Earth3, EC-Earth3-Veg—based on their demonstrated effectiveness for the California region, as determined through process-based and statistical metric56. These models were also chosen because high-resolution (~3 km) statistically downscaled projections are available. These CMIP6 GCMs typically operate at coarse spatial resolutions, they do not adequately capture fine-scale geographic variation critical for accurate chill estimation. Therefore, we used downscaled outputs of these selected GCMs generated through the Localized Constructed Analogs, version 2 (LOCA2)57, which provide improved spatial resolution (3 km). The downscaled, gridded datasets for the period 2015–2100 are publicly available in NetCDF format through Cal-Adapt (https://cal-adapt.org/data/download/).For future projections, we used the high-emission scenario, Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP 5–8.5), which assumes continued growth in greenhouse gas emissions throughout the century58. Although multiple scenarios are commonly assessed in climate impact studies, we selected SSP 5–8.5 to enable a direct comparison with earlier winter chill assessments that used the highest emissions scenarios available at the time to establish the earliest plausible timeline of chill decline. Consistent with this objective, SSP 5–8.5 is used here to evaluate whether the upper-bound projected decline in winter chill is sufficient to explain the observed historical trends. Extending this analysis to multiple SSPs to assess scenario-dependent uncertainty remains an important direction for future work.We clipped these CMIP6 projected chill for each crop-growing region in Southern California and computed the average annual chill accumulation for each crop. We compared the number of years with insufficient chill in observed data and historical projections for the same period (1981–2014) to examine whether CMIP6 projections under- or overestimate the observed decline in chill. Also, we examined whether the continuous decline in chill is similar across all counties.The chill sufficiency thresholds for pistachio, walnut, cherry, and plum were adopted from Pope5. For each crop, chilling requirements vary substantially across cultivars, locations, years, and experimental methods, particularly for stone fruits59. We use a crop-specific chill threshold defined as the median across all varieties of a given crop reported in Pope5. We use the median of CP, since it is less sensitive to extremes and enables consistent regional-scale comparisons in the absence of county-level varietal data. These thresholds are treated as representative benchmarks rather than fixed physiological limits. Consequently, our results are interpreted in terms of increasing frequency and proximity of episodic chill insufficiency driven by interannual variability, rather than the precise timing of persistent threshold crossings inferred from long-term trends. While cultivars with higher-than-median chill requirements may face greater vulnerability and lower-chill cultivars may experience reduced risk, our objective is not to predict cultivar-specific outcomes but to characterize regional-scale chill risk and its temporal evolution under observed conditions, climate projections, and forecasts.To evaluate interannual predictive performance, we compared how many years in the observational record were correctly captured by ECMWF forecasts. Forecast accuracy was evaluated at two thresholds: predictions were considered correct if the predicted CP were within 10% or within 5% of the observed NDJF totals, since large overestimates could misrepresent chill sufficiency. Although crops may tolerate up to a 20% shortfall, we adopted this conservative threshold to offer more robust forecasts of chill conditions25.We evaluated the accuracy of chill forecasts derived from bias-corrected ECMWF hindcasts initialized on the first day of each month (November through February), using both daily and monthly mean products. The analysis included forecasts with lead times ranging from 1 to 4 months, depending on initialization month. Forecast skill in predicting chill was assessed at a shorter 1-month lead, with February forecasts first, followed by comparing longer lead time forecasts.We performed this analysis at the crop-growing region level in Southern California (<37° latitude) by clipping the chill data to each region and calculating regional averages. Also, we performed the analysis at the individual grid level. Additionally, we calculated the ACC for each grid between the ECMWF-derived chill and that computed from observations.We also assessed the extent to which interannual variability in winter chill accumulation is influenced by the El Niño–Southern Oscillation (ENSO). To do this, we used the ONI from NOAA’s Climate Prediction Center (CPC), which is based on three-month rolling averages of sea surface temperature (SST) anomalies in the Niño 3.4 region (5° N–5° S, 120°–170° W), derived from the Extended Reconstructed SST dataset, version 5 (ERSST.v5) (https://origin.cpc.ncep.noaa.gov/products/analysis_monitoring/ensostuff/ONI_v5.php). We then computed both lead and lag correlations between the Niño 3.4 index and CP to evaluate the temporal relationship between ENSO phases and chill variability.

Data availability
              
              We used several publicly available datasets in this study. GRIDMET meteorological data are available from the Northwest Knowledge Network (https://www.northwestknowledge.net/metdata/data/). CMIP6 downscaled climate projections (LOCA2) were obtained via the Intake ESM catalog (https://cadcat.s3.amazonaws.com/cae-collection.json). Crop distribution data were obtained from the USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Cropland Data Layer (https://croplandcros.scinet.usda.gov/) as GeoTIFF files and processed into crop-specific spatial masks using ArcGIS Pro. ECMWF seasonal forecast data (daily and monthly products) were obtained from the Copernicus Climate Data Store (https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/). All climate and crop datasets were spatially aligned on a common latitude–longitude grid (WGS84), and crop-specific grid cells were used to extract climate variables for subsequent analysis. Processed datasets used to generate the figures are publicly available on Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20058559. Figure-specific datasets include GRIDMET-derived chill time series, CMIP6 historical and SSP585 projections, ECMWF bias-corrected and monthly-derived chill products, and crop distribution and location datasets used for spatial aggregation and visualization.
            Code availability
              
              All chill computations were performed in R (v4.3.3) using open-source geospatial and climate analysis libraries. Specifically, chill calculations for GRIDMET, ECMWF, and CMIP6 datasets were implemented using the chillR package, applying the Dynamic Model after conversion of daily temperature inputs to hourly temperature using chillR::make_hourly_temps. NetCDF handling and supporting data processing steps within R were performed using the ncdf4, terra, dplyr, and tidyr packages. R workflows were parallelized using furrr and future for efficient grid-cell-level computation. All other data processing, geospatial workflows, and analysis pipelines were implemented in Python (v3.11.11). This included GRIDMET, ECMWF, and CMIP6 preprocessing, spatial mapping, observational–model comparison workflows, crop-specific time series computation, and statistical analyses. Python-based workflows relied on open-source packages including NumPy, Pandas, GeoPandas, Xarray, SciPy, Matplotlib, and Shapely. All model-specific preprocessing steps are implemented in the scripts corresponding to each analysis component. Final integration of R-derived chill outputs and Python-processed datasets was used to generate all reported results, including regional (south of 37° N) and grid-level performance metrics. All scripts were developed in-house and follow a fully reproducible workflow using consistent input datasets. All code used in this study is publicly available at: https://github.com/pkj002/califo_work/tree/main/CommSust.

items.title: VOIHotels e WWF insieme per un’ospitalità più attenta alla natura
items.description:
Prosegue nel 2026 la collaborazione fra il WWF Italia e VOIhotels, la divisione alberghiera di Alpitour World
The post VOIHotels e WWF insieme per un&
items.body: In evidenza WWF Italia: 60 anni per la natura In evidenza Il Panda Siamo Noi In evidenza L'orso bruno marsicano In evidenza Adotta una specie Prosegue nel 2026 la collaborazione fra il WWF Italia e VOIhotels, la divisione alberghiera di Alpitour World. Il nuovo ciclo di attività si sviluppa nell’ambito di “Destinazione Natura”, il nome attribuito alla collaborazione per il 2026 tra WWF Italia e VOIhotels, che raccoglie sotto un’unica cornice le principali aree di lavoro condivise, dalla gestione della plastica alle attività educative e al coinvolgimento degli ospiti. Con i brand VOIhotels e VRetreats, la catena alberghiera conta oggi 27 strutture tra resort e hotel in Italia e all’estero e accoglie oltre 1,6 milioni di ospiti all’anno. La collaborazione con il WWF Italia, che coinvolge le 19 strutture VOIhotels sul territorio italiano, contribuisce a rafforzare la responsabilità aziendale nelle pratiche quotidiane e nel dialogo con un pubblico ampio e diversificato. Le priorità Tra le priorità di quest’anno è previsto l’avvio di un lavoro strutturato sul tema plastica e packaging. Il WWF Italia affiancherà VOIhotels in un percorso volto alla definizione di una policy aziendale e di un piano d’azione con obiettivi misurabili per ridurne l’impronta plastica e contrastare i rischi di dispersione in natura. L’iniziativa agirà su più livelli, coinvolgerà diverse funzioni aziendali e sarà accompagnata da momenti di formazione interna e dalla condivisione di contenuti informativi che coinvolgeranno anche gli ospiti. La collaborazione prosegue nel 2026 anche sul fronte educativo, con il rafforzamento delle attività dedicate ai più giovani. Dopo le esperienze avviate lo scorso anno, è prevista la realizzazione presso il VOI Tanka Village in Sardegna di uno spazio dedicato all’incontro autentico con la natura. Non una semplice area giochi, ma un percorso sensoriale tra specie autoctone e installazioni interattive. Qui i giovani ospiti potranno imparare a leggere il territorio, scoprendo i segreti della flora e della fauna locale e l’importanza della conservazione, per tornare a casa con una nuova consapevolezza e apprendendo il rispetto per il mondo naturale. Operatività e sensibilizzazione Nel corso della stagione 2026, VOIhotels attiverà inoltre iniziative di raccolta fondi che offriranno agli ospiti la possibilità di sostenere volontariamente le attività istituzionali del WWF Italia, diventando parte attiva dell’impegno ambientale della catena alberghiera. Nel complesso, la collaborazione si articola come un percorso di lavoro che unisce operatività e sensibilizzazione, con un’attenzione particolare alla definizione di azioni concrete. L’obiettivo è fare  del viaggio anche un’occasione di consapevolezza, portando contenuti legati alla tutela della natura all’interno dell’esperienza di soggiorno, in modo coerente con l’ospitalità.

items.title: Omaggio a Sir David Attenborough
items.description: David Attenborough compie 100 anni l’8 maggio 2026: un traguardo che non riguarda solo la sua straordinaria longevità, ma anche l’impatto che ha avuto
items.body:
Home
                    
                            
                            
                                                                    
                        News ed Eventi
                    
                            
                            
                                                                    
                        News
                    
                            
                            
                                                Omaggio a Sir David Attenborough

Omaggio a Sir David Attenborough

Tag:
                                                                            Informazione ambientale                                                                    
                                                    
                                    
            
            
            
                
                                            Pubblicato il:
                        
                            8 Maggio 2026

Condividi

Condividi
